title: Microsoft SharePoint Server Authenticated RCE via Improper Input Validation Patched in March 2026 Patch Tuesday — On-Premises Deployments at Risk (CVE-2026-26106)

sources.name: Microsoft Support — KB5002843 SharePoint Server Subscription Edition Security Update
BleepingComputer — Microsoft March 2026 Patch Tuesday fixes 2 zero-days, 79 flaws
Cisco Talos — Microsoft Patch Tuesday for March 2026 Snort rules and prominent vulnerabilities
CybersecurityNews — Microsoft Patch Tuesday March 2026 — 78 Vulnerabilities Fixed
Windows News — Microsoft Patches Critical SharePoint RCE Vulnerability CVE-2026-26106
Akamai Security Research — SharePoint RCE Exploitations Detections and Mitigations
Stefan Gossner Blog — March 2026 CU for SharePoint Server 2019
Windows Forum — March 10 2026 Patch: Fix for SharePoint CVE-2026-26106 On-Premises

timeline.event: Microsoft discloses CVE-2026-26106 as part of March 2026 Patch Tuesday and releases security updates for all affected SharePoint Server versions
KB5002843 and related cumulative updates published for SharePoint Server Subscription Edition, 2019, and 2016
Security researchers begin publishing analysis of the SharePoint RCE vulnerability and its exploitation potential
No confirmed active exploitation in the wild reported as of this date; vulnerability remains unpatched on many on-premises deployments

simple_summary.what_happened: Microsoft patched a serious security flaw in its on-premises SharePoint Server product that could allow attackers who already have basic user access to run malicious code on the server. The vulnerability was fixed in Microsoft's March 2026 monthly security update, but organizations that haven't applied the patch remain at risk. SharePoint Online (cloud version) is not affected.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations running Microsoft SharePoint Server 2016, 2019, or Subscription Edition on their own infrastructure (on-premises). Any company, government agency, or institution that hosts its own SharePoint environment and grants users Site Member permissions or higher is potentially at risk. SharePoint Online users hosted by Microsoft are not affected.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: The attacker obtains valid credentials for a SharePoint site, either by phishing, credential theft, or using an existing legitimate user account. Step 2: The attacker logs into the SharePoint environment with at least Site Member-level permissions. Step 3: The attacker crafts a specially formed malicious HTTP request that exploits the improper input validation flaw in SharePoint's processing logic. Step 4: The server processes the malicious input and executes attacker-controlled code under the SharePoint application pool account — a highly privileged service account. Step 5: The attacker gains a foothold on the SharePoint server, potentially deploying web shells, stealing sensitive documents, exfiltrating credentials, or moving laterally through the corporate network.
what_you_should_do: Apply the March 2026 Patch Tuesday security updates for your SharePoint Server version immediately: KB5002843 for Subscription Edition, and the corresponding updates for SharePoint 2019 and 2016
Audit SharePoint Site Member and higher permission grants — revoke access for inactive accounts and external users who no longer require access
Search IIS and SharePoint ULS logs for unusual large POST requests to SharePoint admin or layout pages that may indicate exploitation attempts
Monitor for unexpected child processes (cmd.exe, powershell.exe) spawned by the SharePoint worker process (w3wp.exe) using your EDR solution
Consider restricting network access to SharePoint servers to trusted IP ranges as a temporary compensating control until patching is complete
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: SharePoint stores some of an organization's most sensitive data — strategic documents, HR files, financial records, and internal communications. An attacker who successfully exploits this vulnerability gains the ability to silently exfiltrate all of that data or install persistent malware. While authentication is required, attackers frequently obtain valid credentials through phishing campaigns. Once they have any user account with standard permissions, this vulnerability becomes a direct path to server-level control. The risk is especially high for organizations with internet-facing SharePoint portals accessible to contractors, partners, or remote staff.

tactics.description: Attackers use legitimately obtained credentials (phished, purchased, or compromised) with at minimum Site Member permissions to authenticate to SharePoint before exploiting the vulnerability
The core vulnerability — improper input validation in SharePoint's server-side processing allows authenticated attackers to execute arbitrary code by sending a crafted HTTP request
Following successful exploitation, attackers can deploy ASPX web shells into SharePoint directories to maintain persistent remote access without requiring credentials
Attacker leverages their foothold to enumerate and exfiltrate sensitive documents, files, and internal data stored within the SharePoint environment
From the SharePoint server, attackers can pivot to other internal systems using the compromised SharePoint service account's network access

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on post-authentication anomalies in IIS logs, unusual child process spawning from the SharePoint worker process, and web shell deployment indicators. Since exploitation requires authentication, hunting for unusual activity from valid accounts is critical.

log_sources.description: Monitor for unusually large or malformed POST requests to SharePoint administrative pages, layouts, or web service endpoints. Exploitation crafts specifically structured input that deviates from normal request patterns.
SharePoint's own logging system records server-side processing errors and exceptions that may indicate failed or successful exploitation attempts via improper input handling.
Alert on child processes (cmd.exe, powershell.exe, rundll32.exe, mshta.exe) spawned by w3wp.exe (SharePoint's IIS worker process). This is a high-fidelity indicator of post-exploitation activity.
Monitor for logon events associated with the SharePoint service account outside of normal business hours, and for new file creation events in SharePoint web directories.
Alert on creation of new .aspx, .ascx, .ashx, or .asmx files in SharePoint virtual directories — a common indicator of web shell deployment following exploitation.
Monitor for unusual outbound connections from SharePoint servers to external IPs or cloud services — potential data exfiltration or C2 beaconing after successful compromise.

what_to_look_for.description: The SharePoint IIS worker process should not normally spawn command-line shells. This is a near-certain indicator of post-exploitation code execution.

what_to_look_for.indicator: Large or malformed POST requests to SharePoint layout pages
what_to_look_for.description: Exploit payloads are delivered via crafted HTTP POST requests. Look for requests with unusual content lengths or unexpected character sequences in request bodies.
what_to_look_for.log_field: cs-method='POST' AND cs-uri-stem LIKE '%/_layouts/%' AND cs-bytes > 10000

what_to_look_for.indicator: New ASPX files created in SharePoint web directories
what_to_look_for.description: Web shell deployment is a common post-exploitation step. New .aspx files appearing in SharePoint virtual directories outside of deployment processes are suspicious.

what_to_look_for.indicator: SharePoint service account making unusual outbound connections
what_to_look_for.description: If the SharePoint app pool account initiates outbound connections to external hosts, this may indicate a reverse shell or data exfiltration activity.

immediate_actions.action: Apply March 2026 Patch Tuesday Security Updates
immediate_actions.description: Immediately deploy the security updates for all affected SharePoint versions. For SharePoint Server Subscription Edition, install KB5002843. For SharePoint 2019 and 2016, deploy the corresponding March 2026 CUs available via Microsoft Update Catalog or WSUS. Test in non-production first if possible, but expedite deployment on internet-facing systems.

immediate_actions.action: Identify and Prioritize Internet-Facing SharePoint Instances
immediate_actions.description: Immediately identify all SharePoint deployments accessible from the internet or from external contractors/partners. These are highest risk and should receive emergency patching priority. Consider temporarily restricting access to trusted IP ranges until patched.

Review all accounts with Site Member or higher permissions in SharePoint. Revoke access for inactive accounts, former employees, and external users who no longer need access. Apply principle of least privilege — not every user needs Site Member access.

immediate_actions.action: Hunt for Web Shells in SharePoint Directories
immediate_actions.description: Search for recently created or modified .aspx, .ascx, .ashx, and .asmx files in SharePoint virtual directories. Any file created or modified since March 10, 2026, outside of scheduled deployments should be investigated.

immediate_actions.action: Review IIS and SharePoint Logs for Exploitation Indicators
immediate_actions.description: Search IIS logs for large POST requests to SharePoint layout pages and ULS logs for processing errors that may indicate exploitation attempts. Review logs from the past 30 days.
